facts.text: Louis Armstrong's gravelly voice and trumpet playing made him the first true jazz superstar, earning him the nickname 'Satchmo.'
The Selmer Mark VI saxophone, produced from 1954 to 1974, is considered the holy grail of jazz saxophones and commands prices over $10,000 on the vintage market.
Miles Davis released 'Kind of Blue' on August 17, 1959 — it became the best-selling jazz album of all time.
Bebop emerged in the early 1940s as a complex, fast-tempo style pioneered by Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie.
The Village Vanguard in New York City's Greenwich Village has been hosting live jazz since 1935, making it one of the oldest jazz clubs in the world.
Herbie Hancock's 1983 hit 'Rockit' was one of the first songs to bring turntablism and electronic beats into the jazz mainstream.
The ii-V-I chord progression is the most common harmonic movement in jazz and forms the backbone of thousands of standards.
Django Reinhardt pioneered gypsy jazz in the 1930s despite having only two fully functioning fingers on his left hand after a caravan fire.
New Orleans is widely regarded as the birthplace of jazz, where African rhythms, blues, and brass band traditions merged in the early 1900s.
John Coltrane was born on September 23, 1926, in Hamlet, North Carolina.
The Hammond B-3 organ became a staple of jazz organ trios, with Jimmy Smith popularizing its use in the 1950s and 1960s.
Blue Note Records was founded in 1939 by Alfred Lion and Francis Wolff, two German immigrants who loved hot jazz and boogie-woogie.
Scat singing — using nonsense syllables to improvise vocally — was popularized by Louis Armstrong and perfected by Ella Fitzgerald.
Kamasi Washington's 2015 triple album 'The Epic' helped spark a renewed mainstream interest in jazz among younger audiences.
The 12-bar blues form is one of the most fundamental structures in jazz, providing a framework for improvisation.
Preservation Hall in New Orleans' French Quarter has been dedicated to preserving traditional New Orleans jazz since 1961.
Charlie Parker, nicknamed 'Bird,' revolutionized jazz with his virtuosic alto saxophone playing and complex harmonic ideas.
The ride cymbal is the primary timekeeping instrument in a jazz drummer's setup, providing the characteristic 'ding-ding-da-ding' pattern.
Pat Metheny has won 20 Grammy Awards across multiple categories, more than any other jazz artist.
Billie Holiday was born Eleanora Fagan on April 7, 1915, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Cool jazz developed in the late 1940s and early 1950s as a mellower, more relaxed alternative to the intensity of bebop.
The Bach Stradivarius trumpet has been a favorite of jazz trumpet players for decades, prized for its warm, centered tone.
Minton's Playhouse in Harlem was the birthplace of bebop, where jam sessions in the early 1940s attracted Parker, Gillespie, and Monk.
Walking bass lines — steady quarter-note patterns that outline chord changes — are a defining feature of jazz rhythm sections.
Esperanza Spalding became the first jazz artist to win the Grammy for Best New Artist in 2011, beating out Justin Bieber.
Duke Ellington composed over 1,000 pieces during his career, making him one of the most prolific composers in American music.
The Monterey Jazz Festival, first held in 1958, is the longest-running jazz festival in the world.
Thelonious Monk's angular melodies and dissonant harmonies were initially dismissed by critics but later recognized as genius.
Acid jazz emerged in the late 1980s in London, blending jazz with funk, soul, and electronic dance music.
The Fender Rhodes electric piano became iconic in jazz fusion, with its bell-like tones heard on countless 1970s recordings.
Dave Brubeck's 'Take Five,' written in 5/4 time by his saxophonist Paul Desmond, became one of the best-selling jazz singles ever.
Impulse! Records, known as 'The House That Trane Built,' released many of John Coltrane's most important albums.
Robert Glasper has been influential in bridging jazz and hip-hop, collaborating with artists like Kendrick Lamar and Common.
The Great Migration brought jazz from New Orleans northward to cities like Chicago, Kansas City, and New York in the early 20th century.
Comping is the jazz term for the chords and rhythmic accompaniment a pianist or guitarist provides behind a soloist.
Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers served as a training ground for young talent — alumni include Wayne Shorter, Freddie Hubbard, and Wynton Marsalis.
The Cotton Club in Harlem was a famous nightclub during the Prohibition era where Duke Ellington's orchestra was a house band.
Smooth jazz emerged in the late 1970s and 1980s, featuring polished production and accessible melodies over jazz harmonies.
The Zildjian K series cymbals are prized by jazz drummers for their dark, complex, and dry sound.
Nubya Garcia is a British-Guyanese tenor saxophonist who has become a leading figure in London's vibrant young jazz scene.
Louis Armstrong was born on August 4, 1901, in New Orleans, Louisiana.
Blue notes — slightly flattened third, fifth, or seventh scale degrees — give jazz and blues their characteristic emotional quality.
The Newport Jazz Festival was first held in 1954, founded by socialite Elaine Lorillard and jazz promoter George Wein.
Ella Fitzgerald's 'Songbook' series, recording the works of Cole Porter, Gershwin, and others, set the standard for vocal jazz albums.
Latin jazz fuses Afro-Cuban rhythms like the clave with jazz harmony and improvisation.
Snarky Puppy, led by bassist Michael League, blends jazz with funk, rock, and world music and has won multiple Grammys.
Benny Goodman was known as the 'King of Swing' and led one of the first racially integrated musical groups in America.
The call-and-response pattern in jazz has its roots in West African musical traditions and African American church music.
Miles Davis went through more stylistic reinventions than perhaps any other jazz musician, from bebop to cool jazz to fusion to hip-hop-influenced work.
The Wurlitzer electric piano offered a grittier, more biting tone compared to the Rhodes and was favored by some jazz and funk players.
Christian Scott aTunde Adjuah is a New Orleans trumpeter who coined the term 'stretch music' to describe his genre-blending approach.
Kansas City in the 1930s was a wide-open town where jazz thrived in clubs controlled by political boss Tom Pendergast.
Charles Mingus was born on April 22, 1922, in Nogales, Arizona.
Chord substitutions, such as tritone subs, allow jazz musicians to reharmonize melodies and create more colorful harmonic movement.
The ECM record label, founded in 1969 by Manfred Eicher, is known for its pristine sound quality and European jazz aesthetic.
Sarah Vaughan, nicknamed 'The Divine One,' possessed one of the widest vocal ranges in jazz history.
Hard bop emerged in the mid-1950s, adding gospel, blues, and R&B influences back into jazz as a reaction to cool jazz's restraint.
Thundercat (Stephen Lee Bruner) has brought jazz bass virtuosity into modern funk, R&B, and hip-hop productions.
The vibraphone became a prominent jazz instrument thanks to players like Lionel Hampton, Milt Jackson, and Bobby Hutcherson.
Dizzy Gillespie was born John Birks Gillespie on October 21, 1917, in Cheraw, South Carolina.
Frenchmen Street in New Orleans is considered the city's best strip for live jazz, with clubs featuring local musicians nightly.
Free jazz, pioneered by Ornette Coleman in the late 1950s, abandoned fixed chord changes and traditional song forms.
The Steinway piano has been the instrument of choice for many jazz pianists, prized for its rich tone and responsive action.
Count Basie's orchestra was famous for its powerful swing feel, tight ensemble playing, and the leader's minimalist piano style.
Bebop musicians often played at breakneck tempos to discourage less skilled players from sitting in at jam sessions.
Ken Burns' 2001 PBS documentary 'Jazz' traced the history of jazz over 10 episodes and nearly 19 hours.
The Martin Committee trumpet was favored by Miles Davis for its dark, mellow tone during his cool jazz period.
Chick Corea's Return to Forever was one of the most influential jazz fusion bands of the 1970s.
John Coltrane's 'A Love Supreme,' recorded in December 1964, is widely considered one of the greatest jazz albums ever made.
Swing feel — the slight unevenness of eighth notes creating a lilting, bouncy rhythm — is one of jazz's most distinctive qualities.
Birdland, the famous New York jazz club, was named after Charlie 'Bird' Parker and opened on December 15, 1949.
Nina Simone blended jazz, classical, blues, and folk music while using her platform for civil rights activism.
The Yamaha Custom Z saxophone has become popular among modern jazz players for its free-blowing design and projection.
Afro-Cuban jazz was pioneered in the 1940s by Dizzy Gillespie and Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo.
Prestige Records, founded in 1949, recorded early sessions by Miles Davis, John Coltrane, and Thelonious Monk.
Improvisation is the heart of jazz — musicians spontaneously create melodies over chord progressions in real time.
Gregory Porter is known for his rich baritone voice and signature balaclava-style hat worn during performances.
The Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s and 1930s was a cultural flowering that elevated jazz as a serious American art form.
Duke Ellington was born Edward Kennedy Ellington on April 29, 1899, in Washington, D.C.
Drum brushes produce a softer, more textured sound than sticks and are essential for ballads and intimate jazz settings.
Verve Records was founded in 1956 by Norman Granz, who had previously managed Ella Fitzgerald and produced Jazz at the Philharmonic concerts.
The anime series 'Cowboy Bebop' features a jazz and blues-influenced soundtrack by Yoko Kanno that introduced many viewers to jazz.
Modal jazz, as heard on 'Kind of Blue,' uses scales (modes) rather than rapid chord changes as the basis for improvisation.
Norah Jones' debut album 'Come Away with Me' won eight Grammys in 2003 and sold over 27 million copies worldwide.
Bobby McFerrin can create the illusion of singing multiple parts simultaneously using vocal techniques and body percussion.
The clarinet was one of the primary instruments in early New Orleans jazz, played by legends like Sidney Bechet and Benny Goodman.
Thelonious Monk was born on October 10, 1917, in Rocky Mount, North Carolina.
Jazz fusion emerged in the late 1960s when musicians like Miles Davis and Tony Williams blended jazz with rock and electronic elements.
The Blue Note jazz club in New York's Greenwich Village has hosted virtually every major jazz artist since opening in 1981.
Berklee College of Music in Boston is one of the world's premier institutions for jazz education and has produced countless jazz professionals.
Chet Baker was known as the 'James Dean of Jazz' for his good looks, cool trumpet style, and haunting vocal delivery.
The trombone has been a core jazz instrument since the genre's earliest days in New Orleans brass bands.
Columbia Records signed Miles Davis in 1955, beginning one of the most important artist-label relationships in jazz history.
Riverside Records released many classic albums by Thelonious Monk, Bill Evans, and Cannonball Adderley in the 1950s and 1960s.
The North Sea Jazz Festival, held annually in Rotterdam, Netherlands, is one of the largest indoor jazz festivals in the world.
Charlie Parker died on March 12, 1955, at age 34. The coroner estimated his body to be that of a 53-year-old.
Jazz musicians use the term 'changes' to refer to the chord progression of a song.
Ornette Coleman's 1959 album 'The Shape of Jazz to Come' announced the arrival of free jazz and divided the jazz world.
The upright (double) bass provides the harmonic and rhythmic foundation in a traditional jazz ensemble.
Jazz had a significant influence on the civil rights movement, with musicians like Max Roach and Charles Mingus composing protest pieces.
Miles Davis was born on May 26, 1926, in Alton, Illinois, and grew up in East St. Louis.
The Montreux Jazz Festival in Switzerland was founded by Claude Nobs in 1967 and has become one of the world's most prestigious music events.
Wynton Marsalis became the first artist to win Grammys in both jazz and classical music in the same year (1984).
The 'head' of a jazz tune refers to the main melody, which is typically played at the beginning and end of a performance.
Ella Fitzgerald was born on April 25, 1917, in Newport News, Virginia.
Dizzy Gillespie's bent trumpet — caused by someone accidentally sitting on it — became his visual trademark.
The New Orleans Jazz & Heritage Festival (Jazz Fest) has been held annually since 1970, celebrating the city's musical heritage.
Bill Evans' trio recordings at the Village Vanguard in 1961 are considered some of the finest live jazz recordings ever made.
Jazz in Europe gained a distinctive identity through players like Jan Garbarek, who brought Nordic influences to the music.
Art Tatum's virtuosic piano technique was so advanced that classical pianist Vladimir Horowitz went to hear him play in awe.
The standard jazz combo typically consists of a rhythm section (piano, bass, drums) plus one or more horn players.
Wes Montgomery revolutionized jazz guitar by playing melodies with his thumb instead of a pick, creating a warm, distinctive tone.
Prohibition-era speakeasies in the 1920s provided steady work for jazz musicians and helped spread the music across America.
Herbie Hancock was born on April 12, 1940, in Chicago, Illinois.
A jazz standard is a composition that is widely known, performed, and recorded, forming the core repertoire of the genre.
John Coltrane's 'sheets of sound' technique involved playing rapid arpeggios that created a dense, cascading effect.
The Juilliard School in New York has a renowned jazz studies program that has trained many top-tier jazz musicians.
Billie Holiday's 1939 recording of 'Strange Fruit,' a song about lynching, is considered one of the first major protest songs in popular music.
Dixieland jazz — also called traditional jazz or New Orleans jazz — features collective improvisation by the front line instruments.
Lionel Hampton was one of the first jazz musicians to feature the vibraphone as a lead instrument.
The Real Book, an unauthorized collection of lead sheets for jazz standards, became an essential resource for jazz musicians starting in the 1970s.
Benny Goodman's 1938 concert at Carnegie Hall was a landmark event that brought swing jazz to the concert hall.
Jazz hip-hop, blending sampled jazz records with hip-hop beats, was popularized by artists like A Tribe Called Quest and Guru's Jazzmatazz.
The trumpet is perhaps the most iconic jazz instrument, associated with legends from Armstrong to Davis to Gillespie.
Charles Mingus was a bassist, composer, and bandleader whose explosive temperament matched the intensity of his music.
Gypsy jazz, also known as jazz manouche, originated in 1930s Paris and features acoustic guitars, violin, and upright bass.
Count Basie was born William James Basie on August 21, 1904, in Red Bank, New Jersey.
The piano is one of the most versatile instruments in jazz, capable of playing melody, harmony, and rhythm simultaneously.
Dave Brubeck's album 'Time Out' (1959) was the first jazz album to sell over a million copies.
Sonny Rollins is known for his powerful tenor saxophone sound and his practice sessions on the Williamsburg Bridge in New York.
Jazz film scores include memorable soundtracks for movies like 'Anatomy of a Murder' (Ellington) and 'Elevator to the Gallows' (Davis).
Chick Corea was born Armando Anthony Corea on June 12, 1941, in Chelsea, Massachusetts.
The swing era of the 1930s and 1940s made jazz America's popular music, with big bands filling dance halls nationwide.
Wayne Shorter is considered one of the greatest jazz composers of the post-bop era, writing standards like 'Footprints' and 'Speak No Evil.'
Jazz influences in film noir soundtracks helped establish the genre's moody, atmospheric cinematography.
The tenor saxophone became the dominant jazz horn through players like Lester Young, Coleman Hawkins, and John Coltrane.
Art Blakey was born on October 11, 1919, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
Dexter Gordon was one of the first bebop tenor saxophonists and later starred in the jazz film 'Round Midnight' (1986).
The concept of 'trading fours' involves soloists alternating four-bar phrases, creating a musical conversation.
Miles Davis' 'Bitches Brew' (1970) fused jazz with rock and funk, shocking jazz purists but opening new creative territory.
The soprano saxophone gained jazz prominence through Sidney Bechet and was later championed by John Coltrane and Wayne Shorter.
Mary Lou Williams was a pioneering jazz pianist, arranger, and composer who was active from the swing era through the avant-garde.
Jazz musicians often refer to performing as 'blowing,' regardless of whether they play a wind instrument.
Horace Silver's funky, gospel-tinged hard bop compositions like 'Song for My Father' became jazz standards.
The baritone saxophone, played by masters like Gerry Mulligan and Pepper Adams, provides a rich, deep voice in jazz ensembles.
Thelonious Monk's composition 'Round Midnight' is one of the most recorded jazz standards of all time.
Sun Ra claimed to be from Saturn and led his Arkestra in avant-garde jazz performances combining music, costumes, and philosophy.
The 'vamp' in jazz is a repeated musical figure or chord progression used as an introduction or background.
Freddie Hubbard was one of the most technically accomplished trumpet players of the hard bop and post-bop eras.
Jazz education programs now exist at hundreds of universities worldwide, a dramatic change from the music's informal apprenticeship origins.
Benny Goodman was born on May 30, 1909, in Chicago, Illinois.
Lee Morgan's 'The Sidewinder' (1964) became a surprise hit, with its catchy boogaloo groove crossing over to pop audiences.
The flugelhorn, with its darker, mellower tone compared to the trumpet, has been used by Art Farmer, Freddie Hubbard, and Chuck Mangione.
Jazz musicians learn the art of 'comping' — providing rhythmic and harmonic support while a bandmate solos.
McCoy Tyner's powerful, quartal voicings on piano became a defining sound of 1960s jazz through his work with Coltrane.
The first jazz recording is generally considered to be the Original Dixieland Jass Band's 1917 recordings for Victor Records.
Oscar Peterson was called the 'Maharaja of the Keyboard' for his extraordinary technical command of the piano.
Jazz funerals in New Orleans feature a brass band that plays mournful dirges on the way to the cemetery and joyful music on the way back.
Cannonball Adderley's alto saxophone on 'Kind of Blue' provided a soulful counterpoint to Miles Davis' restrained trumpet.
The concept of 'swing' in jazz — a rhythmic feeling that defies precise notation — is essential to authentic jazz performance.
Billie Holiday died on July 17, 1959, at age 44 in New York City.
Joe Henderson's tenor saxophone sound and sophisticated harmonic approach made him one of the most respected players of the post-bop era.
Django Reinhardt was born on January 23, 1910, in Liberchies, Belgium.
The 'fake book' tradition in jazz provides simplified charts showing melody and chord symbols for hundreds of standards.
Lester Young's light, airy tenor saxophone style was a major influence on cool jazz and earned him the nickname 'Pres' (President).
The Hammond B-3's Leslie speaker, with its rotating horn, creates the distinctive swirling sound beloved in jazz organ music.
Ahmad Jamal's use of space and dynamics in his piano trio format directly influenced Miles Davis' approach to music.
Stan Getz popularized bossa nova jazz in America with his 1964 collaboration with João Gilberto, 'Getz/Gilberto.'
Jazz has significantly influenced hip-hop production, with producers sampling classic jazz records for beats and atmosphere.
The alto saxophone was Charlie Parker's instrument of choice, and his playing on it redefined what was possible in jazz.
Keith Jarrett's 'The Köln Concert' (1975) is the best-selling solo jazz album and best-selling piano album in history.
John Coltrane died on July 17, 1967, from liver cancer at age 40.
A 'lick' in jazz is a short musical phrase that a musician has practiced and can insert into an improvisation.
Cassandra Wilson is known for her deep contralto voice and innovative approach to reinterpreting jazz standards and pop songs.
The Harmon mute, used by Miles Davis on recordings like 'Round About Midnight,' creates an intimate, whispery trumpet sound.
Jazz clubs often operate on a system of 'sets' — typically two or three performances per evening with breaks between them.
Herbie Hancock's 'Head Hunters' (1973) blended jazz with funk and became one of the first jazz albums to achieve platinum sales.
The bass clarinet has become increasingly prominent in modern jazz through players like Eric Dolphy and Bass Clarinet Choir experiments.
Dave Brubeck was born on December 6, 1920, in Concord, California.
The concept of 'sheets of sound,' coined by critic Ira Gitler, describes Coltrane's rapid, cascading streams of notes.
Milt Jackson's vibraphone playing with the Modern Jazz Quartet combined cool sophistication with deep blues feeling.
Jazz in Japan has a passionate following, with Tokyo alone having hundreds of dedicated jazz bars and listening rooms.
The 'turnaround' is a chord progression at the end of a section that leads back to the beginning, essential in jazz form.
Pat Metheny was born on August 12, 1954, in Lee's Summit, Missouri.
Ellis Marsalis Jr. was the patriarch of the Marsalis family jazz dynasty, which includes sons Wynton, Branford, Delfeayo, and Jason.
The 'woodshed' is jazz slang for practicing intensively — as in 'going to the woodshed' to work on technique.
Betty Carter was known as the 'Godmother of Jazz' for her uncompromising artistry and mentorship of young musicians.
Lennie Tristano was a blind pianist who pioneered free improvisation and influenced the cool jazz movement.
The New Orleans second line is a street parade tradition where musicians and dancers follow a brass band through the city.
Clifford Brown, despite dying at age 25 in a car accident, left a lasting legacy as one of jazz's greatest trumpet players.
Jazz arrangements often feature a 'shout chorus' — a climactic, high-energy ensemble passage near the end of a piece.
Pharoah Sanders, a disciple of Coltrane, became known for his intense, spiritually-charged tenor saxophone playing.
The brushes technique on drums involves swirling wire brushes on the snare drum head to create a soft, shimmering sound.
Ella Fitzgerald won 13 Grammy Awards during her career and is widely regarded as the 'First Lady of Song.'
The concept of 'blowing over changes' means improvising a melody line that fits the underlying chord progression.
Max Roach was a pioneering bebop drummer who demonstrated that drums could be a melodic and compositional instrument.
Branford Marsalis has had success in jazz, pop (touring with Sting), and as bandleader on The Tonight Show.
The pentatonic scale — a five-note scale — is one of the most commonly used melodic devices in jazz improvisation.
Louis Armstrong died on July 6, 1971, in his home in Queens, New York.
Buddy Rich was considered one of the most technically gifted drummers ever, known for his incredible speed and power.
A 'shed session' in jazz refers to an extended, focused practice session designed to internalize new musical concepts.
Archie Shepp was a leading figure in the free jazz and avant-garde movements of the 1960s, blending jazz with African music.
The Rhodes electric piano, officially called the Fender Rhodes, was invented by Harold Rhodes and became ubiquitous in 1970s jazz-funk.
Ron Carter has appeared on over 2,200 recorded sessions, making him one of the most recorded bassists in history.
'Giant Steps' by John Coltrane features a complex chord progression with major thirds moving in unexpected patterns, challenging improvisers.
The organ trio format — Hammond B-3 organ, guitar, and drums — was popularized by Jimmy Smith in the late 1950s.
Jazz standards like 'Autumn Leaves,' 'All the Things You Are,' and 'Stella by Starlight' form the core language every jazz musician must know.
Tito Puente, known as the 'King of Latin Jazz,' brought Latin percussion and big band arrangements into the jazz mainstream.
Miles Davis died on September 28, 1991, from pneumonia, respiratory failure, and a stroke.
The concept of 'voice leading' — smooth movement between chord tones — is a crucial skill for jazz pianists and guitarists.
Lee Konitz was an alto saxophonist known for his cool, cerebral improvisational approach, never playing the same solo twice.
Jazz musicians often quote melodies from other songs during solos, a practice that shows musical wit and deep repertoire knowledge.
The flugelhorn's conical bore gives it a wider, darker tone than the cylindrical-bored trumpet.
Cedar Walton was a prolific hard bop pianist whose compositions, like 'Bolivia' and 'Mosaic,' became jazz jam session staples.
The term 'jazz' likely evolved from the slang word 'jasm,' meaning energy or vigor, in early 20th century America.
Jimmy Smith's album 'The Sermon!' (1959) helped establish the Hammond B-3 organ as a legitimate jazz instrument.
Eric Dolphy was a multi-instrumentalist (alto sax, flute, bass clarinet) who pushed jazz toward the avant-garde before dying at age 36.
The blue note in jazz creates a tension between major and minor tonality that gives the music its emotional depth.
Duke Ellington died on May 24, 1974, in New York City at age 75.
Hank Mobley's tenor saxophone playing on Blue Note recordings epitomized the hard bop style of the late 1950s and 1960s.
A 'chorus' in jazz refers to one complete cycle through the chord progression — soloists typically play multiple choruses.
Carla Bley is a composer, pianist, and bandleader whose works range from avant-garde jazz to large-scale orchestral pieces.
The walking bass line technique requires bassists to create flowing lines of quarter notes that outline the harmony.
Stéphane Grappelli, along with Django Reinhardt, founded the Quintette du Hot Club de France and pioneered jazz violin.
The concept of 'playing outside' in jazz means deliberately using notes that fall outside the expected harmony for creative tension.
Grant Green was a guitarist known for his single-note, blues-drenched approach to jazz, recording extensively for Blue Note.
Kamasi Washington studied at UCLA's jazz program before developing his epic, orchestral approach to modern jazz.
The plunger mute on trumpet or trombone creates a 'wah-wah' effect commonly heard in big band and Ellington arrangements.
Art Pepper's alto saxophone playing combined the cool jazz aesthetic with raw emotional intensity and autobiographical honesty.
Jazz has been called 'America's classical music' — a uniquely American art form born from the fusion of African and European traditions.
The 'rhythm changes' — chord progressions based on Gershwin's 'I Got Rhythm' — are among the most commonly used forms in jazz.
Don Cherry's pocket trumpet and world music influences helped shape the free jazz movement of the 1960s.
Coleman Hawkins' 1939 recording of 'Body and Soul' established the tenor saxophone as a major jazz solo instrument.
The Zildjian cymbal company was founded in Constantinople in 1623, and their instruments remain essential in jazz drumming.
Kenny Clarke is credited with shifting the jazz drummer's timekeeping role from the bass drum to the ride cymbal.
Ella Fitzgerald died on June 15, 1996, at age 79 in Beverly Hills, California.
The baritone saxophone provides a rich, deep foundation in big bands and has been featured in small groups by Gerry Mulligan.
Bobby Hutcherson's vibraphone playing brought the instrument into the post-bop and avant-garde realms of the 1960s.
The concept of 'melodic development' in jazz solos involves building ideas gradually rather than simply running scales.
Fats Waller was a stride pianist, singer, and entertainer whose playful personality brightened the jazz scene of the 1930s and 1940s.
Blue Note Records' iconic album cover designs by Reid Miles and photography by Francis Wolff are as famous as the music itself.
The New York jazz loft scene of the 1960s and 1970s provided affordable rehearsal and performance spaces for avant-garde musicians.
Jimmy Garrison and Elvin Jones formed one of the most powerful rhythm sections in jazz history as members of Coltrane's classic quartet.
Pharoah Sanders was born Farrell Sanders on October 13, 1940, in Little Rock, Arkansas.
The 'sock cymbal' evolved into the modern hi-hat, becoming a crucial component of the jazz drum set.
Paul Chambers' bass playing on 'Kind of Blue' demonstrated how the upright bass could be both supportive and melodically inventive.
Jazz in the USSR was seen as subversive by the government, yet it thrived underground as a symbol of freedom.
The 'contrafact' technique involves writing a new melody over the chord changes of an existing song, a common practice in bebop.
Dexter Gordon was the first jazz musician nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actor, for his role in 'Round Midnight.'
The flute gained popularity in jazz through players like Herbie Mann, Hubert Laws, and Eric Dolphy.
Ben Webster's breathy, romantic tenor saxophone ballad style made him one of the most beloved soloists of the swing era.
International Jazz Day is celebrated on April 30 each year, established by UNESCO in 2011.
The 'flatted fifth' — the tritone — was a revolutionary interval in bebop, creating harmonic tension central to the style.
Nancy Wilson blurred the lines between jazz and pop with her sophisticated vocal style and wide repertoire.
The guitar's role in jazz evolved from rhythm instrument in big bands to featured solo instrument through Charlie Christian's innovations.
Charlie Christian, who died at age 25, is credited with pioneering the electric guitar as a jazz solo instrument.
Sonny Stitt was a bebop alto and tenor saxophonist who faced constant comparisons to Charlie Parker throughout his career.
The 'tag ending' is a technique where jazz musicians repeat the last few bars of a tune to create a satisfying conclusion.
Joe Pass is considered one of the greatest solo jazz guitarists, known for his virtuosic unaccompanied performances.
The Chicago jazz scene in the 1920s featured musicians like King Oliver and Louis Armstrong, who migrated from New Orleans.
Elvin Jones' polyrhythmic drumming style with Coltrane's quartet set a new standard for jazz drum accompaniment.
Mingus' album 'Mingus Ah Um' (1959) is a masterwork of jazz composition, blending blues, gospel, and classical elements.
Wayne Shorter was born on August 25, 1933, in Newark, New Jersey.
The arrangement is crucial in big band jazz — arrangers like Gil Evans, Billy Strayhorn, and Thad Jones shaped the music's orchestral possibilities.
Woody Shaw was an innovative trumpet player who used wide intervals and unusual harmonic approaches in his improvisations.
George Russell's 'Lydian Chromatic Concept of Tonal Organization' provided the theoretical foundation for modal jazz.
The word 'gig' — now used universally for a performance engagement — originated in jazz circles.
Marian McPartland hosted NPR's 'Piano Jazz' for over 30 years, interviewing and performing with virtually every major jazz pianist.
Tony Williams joined Miles Davis' quintet at age 17, revolutionizing jazz drumming with his explosive, dynamic style.
The 'shout chorus' in big band arranging is a high-energy, tutti passage that typically occurs near the climax of the chart.
Clark Terry's 'mumbles' singing style used wordless vocalizations to create humorous, swinging vocal performances.
The Selmer saxophone company in Paris has been the maker of choice for jazz saxophonists for over a century.
JJ Johnson is widely regarded as the first important bebop trombonist, proving the instrument could play at breakneck tempos.
Billie Holiday's autobiography 'Lady Sings the Blues' (1956) detailed her struggles with addiction and racism.
The 'ballad' tempo in jazz provides an intimate setting where musicians can showcase their most lyrical, emotional playing.
Terri Lyne Carrington became the first woman to win a Grammy for Best Jazz Instrumental Album, for 'Money Jungle: Provocative in Blue.'
The sock cymbal — predecessor to the hi-hat — was played with the foot and evolved as drum kits became more complex.
Gil Evans' orchestral arrangements for Miles Davis on 'Miles Ahead,' 'Porgy and Bess,' and 'Sketches of Spain' redefined jazz orchestration.
Paul Desmond's lyrical alto saxophone tone was once described as sounding like 'a dry martini.'
Red Garland's block chord piano style introduced a new approach to jazz piano voicings in Miles Davis' first great quintet.
The jam session is a cornerstone of jazz culture — musicians gather informally to play, learn, and push each other's boundaries.
Snarky Puppy won the Grammy for Best R&B Performance in 2014 for 'Something' featuring Lalah Hathaway.
The 'head arrangement' is a collectively organized arrangement worked out in rehearsal rather than written down on paper.
Billy Strayhorn, Duke Ellington's longtime collaborator, composed 'Take the A Train,' which became Ellington's theme song.
Jazz musicians use 'transcription' — learning solos by ear from recordings — as a primary method of self-education.
Wes Montgomery died on June 15, 1968, at age 45 from a heart attack in Indianapolis.
The 'Big Easy' — New Orleans' nickname — reflects the city's relaxed attitude that helped nurture jazz's laid-back spirit.
Albert Ayler's primordial, screaming tenor saxophone sound took free jazz to its most extreme sonic territory.
The soprano saxophone, smaller and higher-pitched than the alto, gained prominence through John Coltrane's 1961 recording of 'My Favorite Things.'
Mose Allison was a singer-pianist who blended jazz, blues, and a sardonic wit, influencing musicians from The Who to Van Morrison.
The 'changes' to 'Cherokee' — a fast bebop standard — were used as a test of a musician's harmonic and technical facility.
Don Byas was a tenor saxophonist who bridged the swing and bebop eras before spending most of his career in Europe.
Art Blakey died on October 16, 1990, in New York City at age 71.
The jazz waltz — jazz played in 3/4 time — provides a graceful alternative to the standard 4/4 swing feel.
Dianne Reeves is a five-time Grammy winner for Best Jazz Vocal Album and has been one of jazz's premier vocalists since the 1980s.
Coltrane recorded 'Blue Train' in 1957, his only leader date for Blue Note Records, and it became a defining hard bop album.
The electric bass gradually replaced the upright bass in jazz fusion, with players like Jaco Pastorius expanding its possibilities.
Hampton Hawes was a bebop pianist from Los Angeles whose blues-drenched playing influenced a generation of West Coast jazz players.
'Manteca,' recorded by Dizzy Gillespie in 1947, is considered the first Afro-Cuban jazz standard.
The bass trombone adds weight and depth to the low end of a jazz big band's brass section.
Diana Krall has sold over 15 million albums worldwide, making her one of the best-selling jazz artists of all time.
The 'standard repertoire' in jazz includes songs from Tin Pan Alley, Broadway, and the Great American Songbook.
Yusef Lateef was a pioneer in introducing non-Western instruments and concepts into jazz, including the oboe and bamboo flute.
Count Basie died on April 26, 1984, in Hollywood, Florida, at age 79.
The ride cymbal pattern is the primary timekeeping vehicle in most jazz styles, varying from a gentle 'ting' to a driving pulse.
Jackie McLean's sharp, slightly-above-pitch alto saxophone sound was instantly recognizable and deeply emotional.
Thelonious Monk used silence and space as musical elements, leaving gaps in his playing that were as expressive as the notes.
The bass solo in jazz typically features the bassist playing melodic lines while the rest of the band either drops out or plays softly.
Jaco Pastorius revolutionized the electric bass with his virtuosic technique, harmonics, and fretless tone.
Ambrose Akinmusire is a modern trumpet player who blends jazz with spoken word, electronics, and chamber music elements.
The piano trio — piano, bass, and drums — has been one of jazz's most intimate and enduring ensemble formats.
Toots Thielemans proved the harmonica could be a serious jazz instrument, earning him the nickname 'the greatest harmonica player ever.'
Jazz's influence on anime extends beyond 'Cowboy Bebop' to shows like 'Kids on the Slope' and 'Carole & Tuesday.'
The melodica, a keyboard wind instrument, was used by Augustus Pablo in reggae and occasionally appears in jazz contexts.
Bud Powell is considered the founder of modern jazz piano, translating bebop's horn-like lines to the keyboard.
The first Newport Jazz Festival featured performances by Dizzy Gillespie, Billie Holiday, and Ella Fitzgerald in 1954.
A 'double-time' passage in jazz involves playing at twice the prevailing tempo, creating excitement and energy in a solo.
Shirley Horn was a vocalist and pianist known for her extremely slow ballad tempos that created extraordinary intimacy.
The 'bridge' (or B section) of a jazz standard typically provides harmonic contrast to the A sections in AABA song form.
Julian Lage is a virtuoso guitarist who has brought acoustic guitar back to the forefront of modern jazz.
Louis Armstrong's recordings with his Hot Five and Hot Seven in the late 1920s are considered foundational jazz documents.
The 'crash' cymbal in jazz is used sparingly for accents, unlike in rock where it marks structural boundaries.
Maria Schneider has won multiple Grammys for her large ensemble compositions that blend jazz with contemporary classical music.
Oscar Pettiford was one of the first bassists to adapt the cello for jazz improvisation in the 1950s.
Charles Mingus died on January 5, 1979, in Cuernavaca, Mexico, at age 56.
The flügelhorn solo on Chuck Mangione's 'Feels So Good' (1977) became one of the most recognizable melodies in jazz-pop.
Vijay Iyer has been named multiple times as DownBeat's Jazz Artist and Pianist of the Year for his boundary-pushing work.
The diminished scale — alternating half steps and whole steps — is a common tool for creating tension over dominant chords in jazz.
Billy Eckstine led one of the first bebop big bands in the mid-1940s, featuring Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, and Sarah Vaughan.
The Aebersold play-along series has helped millions of jazz students practice improvisation by providing backing tracks for standards.
Erroll Garner composed 'Misty' in 1954, which became one of the most recorded jazz standards after Johnny Mathis' hit version.
Brad Mehldau has brought influences from Radiohead and Nirvana into his jazz piano trio performances.
The 'break' in jazz is a brief section where the rhythm section stops playing, leaving a soloist to play unaccompanied.
Roy Haynes, who played with Parker, Coltrane, and Corea across seven decades, is one of the longest-active drummers in jazz history.
The jazz organ has experienced a revival in the 21st century through players like Joey DeFrancesco and Dr. Lonnie Smith.
Wynton Marsalis was born on October 18, 1961, in New Orleans, Louisiana.
The saxophone was invented by Adolphe Sax in the 1840s, but it didn't find its true home until jazz embraced it decades later.
Weather Report, featuring Wayne Shorter and Joe Zawinul, was one of the most successful jazz fusion bands of the 1970s and 1980s.
The concept of 'time feel' in jazz refers to how a musician places notes relative to the beat — slightly ahead, behind, or right on.
Cassandra Wilson won a Grammy for her 1995 album 'New Moon Daughter,' which reimagined classic songs through a jazz lens.
The alto flute and bass flute have been used in jazz for their darker, more haunting tonal qualities compared to the concert flute.
Art Farmer's flugelhorn sound on albums like 'Modern Art' helped establish the instrument as a lyrical jazz voice.
Louis Armstrong appeared in over 30 films during his career, bringing jazz to movie audiences worldwide.
The 'pedal point' technique in jazz involves holding a single bass note while the harmony changes above it, creating tension.
McCoy Tyner was born on December 11, 1938, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Cécile McLorin Salvant won the Thelonious Monk International Jazz Vocals Competition in 2010, launching her career as a major jazz vocalist.
The '52nd Street' scene in midtown Manhattan during the 1940s and 1950s featured dozens of jazz clubs within a few blocks.
Joe Lovano is a tenor saxophonist known for his broad stylistic range, from hard bop to free improvisation.
John McLaughlin's Mahavishnu Orchestra pushed jazz fusion into virtuosic, high-volume territory in the early 1970s.
The 'outro' or 'coda' in jazz provides a composed ending after the final head, often featuring a ritardando or fermata.
Geri Allen was a pianist and composer who blended the traditions of bop with free jazz and contemporary classical music.
The hi-hat cymbal, played with both foot and sticks, provides essential rhythmic texture in jazz drumming.
Dave Holland's bass playing and compositions have been central to both avant-garde and mainstream jazz since the late 1960s.
Jazz slang gave the English language words like 'cool,' 'hip,' 'gig,' 'jam,' and 'riff.'
Abbey Lincoln was a vocalist and civil rights activist whose album 'We Insist!' with Max Roach addressed racial injustice.
The Conn 10M 'Naked Lady' tenor saxophone was favored by Lester Young and many swing-era tenor players.
Christian McBride is a bassist, composer, and radio host who has been one of jazz's most in-demand musicians since the 1990s.
The 'lay back' phrasing technique involves placing notes slightly behind the beat, creating a relaxed, unhurried feel.
Ornette Coleman was born on March 9, 1930, in Fort Worth, Texas.
Herbie Hancock's 'Maiden Voyage' (1965) used suspended chords and oceanic imagery to create one of jazz's most atmospheric albums.
The jazz flute gained prominence in bossa nova recordings, adding a light, airy texture to the Brazilian-jazz fusion.
Marcus Miller's bass playing and producing helped shape Miles Davis' 1980s comeback, particularly on the album 'Tutu.'
Avishai Cohen (the Israeli bassist) has blended jazz with Middle Eastern and Mediterranean musical traditions.
The 'front line' in traditional jazz refers to the melody instruments — typically trumpet, clarinet, and trombone.
Hank Jones, the eldest of the Jones brothers (with Thad and Elvin), was a master of elegant, refined bebop piano.
Chick Corea died on February 9, 2021, at age 79, leaving behind over 90 albums as leader or co-leader.
The 'pocket' in jazz refers to the rhythmic groove established by the bass and drums that makes the music feel good.
Joshua Redman, son of saxophonist Dewey Redman, turned down Yale Law School to pursue jazz full-time.
Gretsch drums have been favored by jazz drummers for their warm, resonant tone and classic round badge aesthetic.
The 'double bass' got its jazz nickname 'doghouse bass' from early musicians who thought it resembled a large doghouse.
Billy Strayhorn was born on November 29, 1915, in Dayton, Ohio.
Kurt Rosenwinkel's guitar playing has been influential in shaping the modern jazz guitar sound since the late 1990s.
The 'three-horn front line' of trumpet, alto sax, and tenor sax became a standard hard bop ensemble configuration.
Makaya McCraven is a Chicago-based drummer and producer who creates jazz through live improvisation remixed in the studio.
Roy Hargrove was a trumpet player who bridged straight-ahead jazz and neo-soul before his death at age 49 in 2018.
The piano 'comping' style in jazz involves irregular rhythmic accents that complement and respond to the soloist.
Trombone Shorty (Troy Andrews) carries on the New Orleans tradition, blending jazz, funk, and rock in his high-energy performances.
Oscar Peterson was born on August 15, 1925, in Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
The 'cup mute' on brass instruments produces a more subtle, covered sound used in both big band and small group settings.
Terence Blanchard has composed scores for over 50 films, including most of Spike Lee's movies, while maintaining a serious jazz career.
The 'ghost note' in jazz drumming is a very soft snare hit that adds rhythmic nuance and texture to a groove.
Charles Lloyd's quartet with Keith Jarrett in the 1960s was one of the first jazz groups to perform at rock festivals.
Jason Moran has served as Artistic Director for Jazz at the Kennedy Center, bringing innovative programming to the institution.
The practice of 'woodshedding' a transcribed solo helps jazz musicians internalize the language and phrasing of the masters.
Cannonball Adderley's 1966 hit 'Mercy, Mercy, Mercy' was a rare jazz single that crossed over to the pop charts.
